summary: Gruber explains why the automated Twitter posting bot for Daring Fireball broke during a server migration in November 2019 and how he finally fixed it three months later. The original script used a Berkeley DB database for tracking which posts had been tweeted, which broke due to Perl version incompatibilities on the new server. He rewrote it to use a simple text file instead, noting that a database was premature optimization all along. He also explains why he maintains his own custom script rather than using third-party services: he wants precise control over tweet formatting, including elegant truncation of long headlines.
key_points: A server migration broke the DF Tootbot because the Berkeley DB file format changed across Perl major versions, making the history log unreadable
Gruber replaced the database with a plain text file after testing showed that scanning a 1-million-line text file takes a fraction of a second on modern hardware
He cites Knuth's 'premature optimization is the root of all evil' as the lesson learned from over-engineering the original solution
He maintains a custom tweeting script rather than using third-party services because he wants exact control over tweet text formatting, including elegant headline truncation
The rewrite also switched from RSS to JSON Feed as the data source and added support for Twitter's 280-character limit
money_quotes: It bothered me because the database wasn't something I could just open in BBEdit and inspect and edit by hand. I'm a text file guy, as you may know.
Reading a simple text file log with tens of thousands of lines is really really fast on a modern computer. It would have been more than fast enough 10 years ago, and it's much faster today.
I hope to write Daring Fireball for a long time to come, but I really doubt I have a million posts in me.

Manually doing anything that should be automated is a bad idea for anyone, but it's a particularly bad idea for someone with a strong tendency to think about only one thing at a time and pretty much forget everything else.
I like having control over such things. Shocker, I know.
key_insight: Premature optimization — choosing a database over a plain text file for a simple log — created a fragile dependency that broke years later, when a text file would have been simpler, faster to debug, and more than performant enough.
